summary: I was born in Wheeling, Illinois in 1994, a small town where my grandparents moved to live with my family after migrating from the Philippines. Wheeling became the backdrop of my childhood, where I played in the park under my lola's watchful eye and spent afternoons watching Jeopardy on my lolo's lap. It was in Wheeling that my grandparents embraced American culture while maintaining their Filipino traditions.

The Philippines is where my mother and her siblings were born and raised, specifically in Manila. My family’s roots there are strong, with my lolo and lola eventually returning there after living in the United States. The culture and traditions from the Philippines have deeply influenced my identity, even as I grew up in America.

Manila, the capital of the Philippines, is where my mother and her five siblings were born and raised. It was from Manila that my mother and her sisters migrated to the United States during the 1980s and 1990s to pursue education and career opportunities. Manila represents the starting point of my family's journey to America and the place where their early lives and education took shape.

The United States is where I was born and raised, specifically in Wheeling, Illinois, and where my family sought new opportunities. My mother and her sisters came here for education and work, and my grandparents followed in 1991 to be close to us. Growing up in the U.S., I experienced a blend of American culture and Filipino heritage, which shaped my identity profoundly.

New York City was where my aunt Vina moved to pursue her Master's degree after finishing her education in the Philippines. It was part of my family's migration story during the 1980s and 1990s, highlighting the pursuit of higher education in America. Though I didn’t live there myself, New York City represents the academic ambitions of my family members abroad.

Jeopardy! was a big part of my childhood memories, especially watching it on my lolo’s lap in Wheeling. It was one of the ways my grandparents immersed themselves in American culture and improved their English. I even remember pronouncing 'Jeopardy' with a Filipino accent before I learned the correct English pronunciation in school.

I have fond memories of the Fourth of July parades during my childhood in the United States. While I enjoyed the American tradition of collecting candy during the celebrations, my home life was also filled with Filipino culture, which created a unique blend of experiences for me. The Fourth of July symbolized my American upbringing alongside my Filipino heritage.

Tagalog was the language my lolo sometimes used, especially when he swore, which could be quite startling to me as a child. It connected me to my Filipino roots and was part of the bilingual environment I grew up in. Though I was born in the U.S., hearing Tagalog at home helped me maintain a link to my family's heritage.

Burger King was a place I often wanted to visit as a child, especially when my lolo picked me up from elementary school. I would try to convince him to buy me a kids meal there, which became a small but cherished part of our bonding. It represents a slice of my American childhood and the simple joys shared with my grandparents.

‘Dahil Sa Iyo’ is a song I remember singing with my lolo during my childhood. It was part of the Filipino music that connected me to my cultural heritage even as I grew up in America. Singing it together was a special way we shared our Filipino identity amidst my American upbringing.
